History & Culture · Ferrocarril al Atlántico · Mile 75

Estación de Juan ViñasCartago Province · 120.1 km / 75 miles from Limón

A station spoke of the Atlantic Railway documentary supplement, gathering the depot, the Stanley Lindo sugar estate, and the cane fields that gave the stop its working life

↩ The Full Line The Depot The Sugar Estate The Town History

Mile 75 on the descent from San José — a station, a sugar mill, and a town built on the cane that filled its boxcars.

About this spoke: Juan Viñas is one of three stations on the Ferrocarril al Atlántico — alongside Cartago and Turrialba — documented in detail as part of this supplement. This page gathers the postcard and photographic record specific to Juan Viñas: the station building itself, the Stanley Lindo sugar estate that anchored the local economy, and the town that grew up around both.
Distance from Limón
120.1 km / 75 miles
Distance from San José
45.5 km / 28¼ miles
Elevation
879 m
Province
Cartago

The Depot

JV-001 CR-AN-AH-FO-004863 candidate · date pending confirmation

Estación de Juan Viñas

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — depot building, front elevation

The station building presents its gable end to the platform, corrugated metal sheathing over a wood frame, with the station name lettered on a black board beneath the roofline and a distance/elevation sign mounted just above the doorway. Two ox-drawn carts, fitted with rubber tires in place of the traditional wooden wheel, stand loaded at the building's flank — a small but telling detail of the transitional period when motor transport and oxcart haulage briefly shared the same yard.

A boxcar numbered 1781 is visible at the left edge of the frame, the same car photographed head-on in the companion view below — confirming both images were taken within moments of each other, from opposite ends of the same siding.

Subject
Estación de Juan Viñas, front/gable elevation
Visible markings
"JUAN VIÑAS" station board; building number "1781" on adjacent boxcar
Companion plate
JV-002 (boxcar 1781, reverse angle)
Reference
Pending — archival accession number to be confirmed
Source: Craig's personal archive, file 004863. Archival reference and date pending confirmation.
JV-002 CR-AN-AH-FO-004862 candidate · date pending confirmation

Furgón 1781 en la Estación

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — boxcar 1781 and distance sign

Shot from trackside looking back toward the depot, this view puts boxcar 1781 squarely in the frame and, just past its roofline, the station's distance sign: San José 28¼ millas — Juan Viñas — Limón 75½ millas, with an elevation of 879 metros given beneath. The figures correspond closely to the FECOSA station archive's cumulative distance of 120.1 km (75 miles) from Limón used elsewhere in this supplement, confirming the same station data from a second, independent primary source.

An automobile is parked trackside at left, and a figure in dark dress walks the platform toward the depot — a small human note in an otherwise infrastructure-focused composition. The wooded hillside rising behind the building is characteristic of the Turrialba–Juan Viñas corridor's steep, cloud-forested terrain.

Subject
Boxcar 1781 and station distance/elevation sign
Sign reading
San José 28¼ mi. · Juan Viñas · Limón 75½ mi. · Elevación 879 metros
Cross-reference
Corroborates FECOSA distance of 120.1 km / 75 mi. from Limón (see line table)
Reference
Pending — archival accession number to be confirmed
Source: Craig's personal archive, file 004862. Archival reference and date pending confirmation.
JV-003 CR-AN-AH-FO-004861 candidate · date pending confirmation

Tren en la Vía, Juan Viñas

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — passenger train stopped trackside

A passenger train idles on a curve cut into the hillside, open-sided coaches crowded with figures leaning from the platforms while children and adults move along the track bed below. An embossed postal stamp is faintly visible in the upper right corner of the card, suggesting this image circulated as a mailed postcard rather than a private snapshot — consistent with the commercial real-photo-postcard trade that documented the line throughout the early twentieth century.

The setting — a steep wooded cut with a single trackside building — matches the general character of the Turrialba–La Junta corridor's terrain, the same stretch the 1998 UCR geological study (cited in the main line supplement) identified as chronically prone to landslide closures during the rainy season.

Subject
Passenger train stopped at a siding, hillside cutting
Format
Real-photo postcard (embossed postal stamp visible, upper right)
Reference
Pending — archival accession number to be confirmed
Source: Craig's personal archive, file 004861. Archival reference and date pending confirmation.

The Sugar Estate — Finca de Stanley Lindo

JV-004 1922 · Manuel Gómez Miralles

Ingenio Azúcar Stanley Lindo

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — sugar mill, twin smokestacks
Ingenio Azúcar Stanley Lindo · Juan Viñas · Sugar Mill, Stanley Lindo

Photographed from an elevated vantage looking down on the mill yard, this card shows the Stanley Lindo sugar estate's main processing complex: twin brick smokestacks rising above a long corrugated-roof mill building, flanked by warehouse sheds and worker housing strung along the access road. An ox-cart sits idle in the yard, and the dirt road leading away from the mill threads past additional outbuildings toward the wider settlement.

The bilingual caption format — Spanish, place name, English — matches the commercial export-economy postcard genre also seen in coffee-finca photography from the same period and region, suggesting Stanley Lindo's operation was photographed and promoted as part of a broader documentary series on Costa Rican plantation agriculture rather than as an isolated commission.

Date
1922
Creator
Manuel Gómez Miralles
Reproduction
Fabio Jiménez Salas, 1986
Reference
CR-AN-AH-FO-002207 (Archivo Nacional)
Source: Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica, Fondo/creator Manuel Gómez Miralles.
JV-005 1922 · Manuel Gómez Miralles

Finca de Mr. Stanley Lindo

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — boyero and yoke of oxen
Finca de Mr. Stanley Lindo · Juan Viñas · Farm of Mr. Stanley Lindo

A boyero stands beside a loaded cart and a yoke of three oxen, the lead pair fitted with substantial horns and the traditional wooden head-yoke construction. He wears a flat cap and what appears to be a leather work apron, holding a goad in his right hand — a standard working portrait of the labor that moved cane from field to mill on the Stanley Lindo estate.

Taken the same year as the mill view (JV-004) and sharing its bilingual caption format, this card forms a deliberate pair: the estate's fixed industrial infrastructure on one side, and the animal-drawn labor that fed it on the other. Together they give Juan Viñas its own boyero-and-finca-economy thread, complementing the field-harvest scene below.

Date
1922
Creator
Manuel Gómez Miralles
Companion plate
JV-004 (Ingenio Azúcar Stanley Lindo)
Reference
CR-AN-AH-FO-002206 (Archivo Nacional)
Source: Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica, Fondo/creator Manuel Gómez Miralles.
JV-006 c. 1905–1915 · publisher unconfirmed

Sugarcan-Farm Juan Viñas

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — cane harvest, ox-drawn carts
Sugarcan-Farm Juan Viñas C. R.

A wide elevated view over a partially harvested cane field, with some half-dozen ox-carts drawn up at the edge of standing cane to be loaded by hand. The misspelled English caption ("Sugarcan-Farm") is typical of the period's commercial postcard printers, who frequently anglicized Costa Rican subjects for the export and tourist trade without close proofreading.

This is almost certainly cane destined for the Stanley Lindo ingenio shown elsewhere on this page — Juan Viñas's economy in this era ran on the same cycle pictured here: oxcart to mill, mill to rail, rail to Limón. High-resolution reproduction processed from the original card.

Estimated date
c. 1905–1915 (half-tone postcard)
Reproduction
Olympus E-M1X / 150–400mm, DxO PhotoLab DeepPRIME XD
Reference
Pending — original card publisher unconfirmed
Source: Craig's personal collection; high-resolution photographic reproduction.

The Town

JV-007 c. 1910–1920 · publisher unconfirmed

Juan Viñas, C. R.

Juan Viñas, Cartago Province — panoramic town view, hand-colored
Juan Viñas, C. R.

A hand-colored panoramic view looking down over the town from an elevated vantage on the surrounding hillside, with the church's twin towers and red tile roof at the composition's center, terraced fields and pasture climbing the slopes beyond, and a pale band of distant water — likely a cloud bank or a glimpse toward the lowlands — along the horizon. The coloring follows the standard period palette of red-orange roof tiles against green hillside and pale blue sky.

This view gives Juan Viñas its civic portrait to set against the working images elsewhere on this page — mill, depot, and cane field — completing the picture of a railway town built almost entirely around a single industry. High-resolution reproduction processed from the original card.

Estimated date
c. 1910–1920 (hand-colored postcard)
Reproduction
Olympus E-M1X / 150–400mm, DxO PhotoLab DeepPRIME XD
Reference
Pending — original card publisher unconfirmed
Source: Craig's personal collection; high-resolution photographic reproduction.

Juan Viñas: A Station Built on Cane

Of the three stations documented in this supplement, Juan Viñas is the one most completely defined by a single enterprise. Where Cartago grew as a colonial seat and provincial capital long before the railway arrived, and Turrialba anchored a more diversified valley economy of coffee, cattle, and cane, Juan Viñas in this period reads almost entirely as company town: depot, mill, and worker housing strung along one road, with the Stanley Lindo ingenio supplying both the cargo that justified the station's existence and much of the built fabric around it.

The photographic record gathered here traces that single thread end to end. Cane is cut and loaded by hand into ox-drawn carts in the fields above town; the same animal labor — a boyero and his yoke of three oxen — moves it toward the mill, whose twin smokestacks and corrugated sheds processed the harvest into refined sugar; and the depot, with its boxcars and distance sign reading 75½ miles to Limón, carried the finished product down the line to the Caribbean port for export. The rubber-tired oxcarts photographed at the depot's flank mark the moment this older animal-powered system began sharing the yard with motor transport, without yet being replaced by it.

The station's own data corroborates the broader line record: the depot sign's distances — 28¼ miles from San José, 75½ from Limón, 879 meters elevation — align closely with the FECOSA archive's cumulative figures of 120.1 km from Limón used in the main line table, giving this spoke an independent primary-source check on the hub's published station data.
